coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence-based immunoassay for the detection of Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae in rice leaf
coredata.dc:creator: Awaludin, Norhafniza
Abdullah, Jaafar
Salam, Faridah
Ramachandran, Kogeethavani
Yusof, Nor Azah
Wasoh, Helmi
coredata.dc:description: The identification of rice bacterial leaf blight disease requires a simple, rapid, highly sensitive, and quantitative approach that can be applied as an early detection monitoring tool in rice health. This paper highlights the development of a turn-off fluorescence-based immunoassay for the early detection of Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae (Xoo), a gram-negative bacterium that causes rice bacterial leaf blight disease. Antibodies against Xoo bacterial cells were produced as specific bio-recognition molecules and the conjugation of these antibodies with graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles was performed and characterized, respectively. The combination of both these bio-probes as a fluorescent donor and metal quencher led to changes in the fluorescence signal. The immunoreaction between AntiXoo-GQDs, Xoo cells, and AntiXoo-AuNPs in the immuno-aggregation complex led to the energy transfer in the turn-off fluorescence-based quenching system. The change in fluorescence intensity was proportional to the logarithm of Xoo cells in the range of 100–105 CFU mL−1. The limit of detection was achieved at 22 CFU mL−1 and the specificity test against other plant disease pathogens showed high specificity towards Xoo. The detection of Xoo in real plant samples was also performed in this study and demonstrated satisfactory results.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bacterial leaf blight disease in rice crop. (a) A view of a BLB-infected paddy field at Sekinchan, Selangor, Malaysia; (b) BLB symptoms depicting yellowish strip lesions along the leaf margins; (c) Bacterial ooze from BLB-infected leaves (IRRI) and (d) scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of Xoo cells.

Bioconjugation of AntiXoo antibodies with GQDs and AuNPs. (a) UV/Vis absorbance of AntiXoo-GQDs conjugation; (b) Fluorescence intensity spectrum of AntiXoo-GQDs conjugation; (c) UV/Vis absorption spectrum of AntiXoo-AuNPs with inset depicting (i) AuNPs, (ii) pallette of AntiXoo-AuNPs after centrifugation, and (iii) concentrated stock of AntiXoo-AuNPs; and (d) The overlapping of fluorescence spectrum (AntiXoo-GQDs) and UV/Vis absorption spectrum (AntiXoo-AuNPs).
Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images of (a) AntiXoo antibodies and (b) AntiXoo-GQDs conjugate. Scale bar = 4 μm.
High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of (a) GQDs (scale bar = 20 nm) and (b) AntiXoo-GQDs (scale bar = 100 nm) at 25Kx magnification with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.
High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of (a) AuNPs and (b) AntiXoo-AuNPs at 25Kx magnification (scale bar = 20 nm) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Scale bar = 20 nm.

Schematic diagram of homogenous turn-off fluorescence graphene quantum dots-based immunoassay using two antibody probes conjugated with GQDs and gold nanoparticles. The change in fluorescence intensity is recorded at the excitation and emission wavelengths of 400 nm and 520 nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (a) Aggregation of bio-probes with Xoo cells in the formation of the ground-state non-fluorescent immunocomplex system and (b) Antibody-antigen affinity binding of P1 (AntiXoo-GQDs) and P2 (AntiXoo-AuNPs) on the surface of Xoo cell. The images are viewed at (a) 10Kx magnification (scale bar = 200 nm) and (b) 30Kx magnification (scale bar = 50 nm) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs, AntiXoo-GQDs, and AntiXoo-GQDs/Xoo/AntiXoo-AuNPs complex system, and (b) fluorescence quenching spectrum of immunocomplex reaction assay with 1 × 105 CFU mL−1 of Xoo cells.
Optimization of conjugates via titration test between AntiXoo-GQDs and AntiXoo-AuNPs with fixed concentration of Xoo cells of 1 × 105 CFU mL−1.

The performance of the turn-off fluorescence GQDs-based immunoassay system. (a) The changes in the fluorescence emission spectra in the absence and presence of various concentrations of X. oryzae pv. oryzae from (a) to (f): 0, 1, 10, 100, 1000, 10000 CFU mL−1; (b) Standard calibration curve depicting the sigmoidal relationship between the fluorescence change efficiency and the log-transformed Xoo cells (PBS buffer system); (c) Specificity test of plant pathogens; SS14 (Xanthomonas sp.), DB2 (Pantoea sp.-papaya), TS3 (Pseudomonas sp.-paddy), and P. o (Pyricularia oryzae-paddy); and (d) Standard calibration curve of leaf matrix. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three replicate measurements at Ex. 400 nm/Em. 520 nm.

The developed symptom of bacterial leaf blight (BLB) disease in rice leaves after the inoculation with X.oryzae pv. oryzae. Samples were harvested at 5 (IL 1), 7 (IL 2) and 14 (IL 3) days after inoculation; IL (inoculated leaf).
The accuracy of Xoo detection in rice leaf using developed GQDs-based immunoassay.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The identification of rice bacterial leaf blight disease requires a simple, rapid, highly sensitive, and quantitative approach that can be applied as an early detection monitoring tool in rice health. This paper highlights the development of a turn-off fluorescence-based immunoassay for the early detection of Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae (Xoo), a gram-negative bacterium that causes rice bacterial leaf blight disease. Antibodies against Xoo bacterial cells were produced as specific bio-recognition molecules and the conjugation of these antibodies with graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles was performed and characterized, respectively. The combination of both these bio-probes as a fluorescent donor and metal quencher led to changes in the fluorescence signal. The immunoreaction between AntiXoo-GQDs, Xoo cells, and AntiXoo-AuNPs in the immuno-aggregation complex led to the energy transfer in the turn-off fluorescence-based quenching system. The change in fluorescence intensity was proportional to the logarithm of Xoo cells in the range of 100–105 CFU mL−1. The limit of detection was achieved at 22 CFU mL−1 and the specificity test against other plant disease pathogens showed high specificity towards Xoo. The detection of Xoo in real plant samples was also performed in this study and demonstrated satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Rice is a staple food in most countries within the Asian region. Countries such as China, India, Indonesia, Bangladesh, and Vietnam are listed as the world's five largest rice producers and consumers, contributing more than 80% of the world's rice supply [1]. Sustainability in rice production is important to meet the global demand for rice. Although the high yield in rice production depends on the quality of rice varieties, other extrinsic factors such as climate change, pests, and disease infection can significantly affect rice yield [2,3]. Bacterial leaf blight disease, also known as BLB, is one of the major diseases in rice crop that is caused by the gram-negative bacterium, Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae [4]. It is one of the most serious and widespread diseases that can reduce approximately 10–20% of rice yield under moderate prevailing conditions and up to 50% under conducive conditions [5]. The infection of X. oryzae pv. oryzae (Xoo) begins at the active tillering stage, in which the bacteria cells invade through the hydathodes or wound of the leaf margin. The symptoms appear as tannish-grey to white-yellowish lesions along the veins of the leaf and followed by wilting and necrosis during the last stage of infection (Fig. 1 ).

Most plant pathologists or farmers rely on naked-eye observations to monitor, screen and identify BLB infection based on the symptoms that are present [6]. However, once the obvious symptoms have appeared, the pathogen colonization is more than 108 CFU mL−1, thereby leading to plant death and subsequently affecting rice yields [7]. Common laboratory methods such as microbial culture, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) have been used to identify and differentiate the pathogens [8–11].

However, these approaches suffer from several drawbacks such as the lack of detection at low concentrations using ELISA in the preliminary stages of infection and time-consuming microbial culture protocols that require more than one week for the isolation and identification of the pathogen. Likewise, DNA-based methods are unable to distinguish live pathogens from the dead ones. Additionally, while the detection using MALDI-TOF and FTIR instruments can provide a fast method of identification [12], these instruments require high costs for maintenance. Therefore, rice disease protection and control management should be equipped with rapid, easy-handling, and precise technologies that can help to detect early infection.

In recent years, the development of highly sensitive detection technologies coupled with the advancement of nanotechnology has been extensively explored as an alternative tool to the conventional methods used in the medical, food safety, environmental, and agricultural sectors [13]. Optical fluorescence biosensors and diagnostic assays using nanomaterials have drawn increasing attention as promising tools for multi-analyte detection [14]. Quantum dots (QDs) are fluorescence nanomaterials that can be used as labelling agents in the development of biosensors and they offer stability against photo-bleaching as compared to organic fluorophores [15]. Semiconductor quantum dots such as cadmium-based QDs (CdSe, CdTe, and CdS) and non-cadmium-based QDs (ZnSe, ZnS, PbS, and PbSe) are fluorescence materials that have been widely studied in imaging and biosensors due to their unique optical properties [16]. However, their advantages are limited by two main issues, namely the strong blinking effect [17] and toxicity in clinical and environmental applications [18].

Recently, many researchers have emphasized the use of graphene-based fluorescence nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for environmental and biological applications [19]. GQDs have unique fluorescence properties and can be synthesized from either bottom-up or top-down approaches [20]. GQDs have attractive properties such as long-term photo-stability, high photoluminescence quantum yield, multi-tuneable colour, biocompatibility (with an aqueous environment for biological use) and more importantly, low toxicity [21]. The interaction of GQD-conjugated biomolecules (DNA, protein, aptamer or antibody) and metal materials under optimized reaction conditions result in a fluorescence signal switch. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) are metal nanoparticles that have remarkable inherent optical properties with a strong localized surface plasmon resonance that drives the electrostatic and hydrogen bond interactions as well as donor-acceptor chemical reactions in the fluorescence signal switch [22]. AuNPs are an excellent metal quencher in fluorescence-based sensors due to their properties such as broad energy absorption bandwidth in the visible range and binding with a wide range of biological ligands or molecules through its unique chemical reactivity [23]. The combination of AuNPs and GQDs as a donor-acceptor pair has been successfully demonstrated in bio-sensing applications with high sensitivity and selectivity [24,25].

In this study, a turn-off nanosensor system using GQDs fluorescence-based immunoassay was developed for the detection of X. oryzae pv. oryzae in rice crop. The immunoreaction of bio-probes and target cells in this quenching system leads to a decrease in fluorescence intensity that is inversely proportional to the increase of Xoo cell concentrations. This sensing system does not require multiple steps that are time-consuming such as the coating, incubation, separation, and washing steps performed in ELISA. Additionally, it provides a sensitive optical signal response due to the immunoreaction complex and energy transfer between probes in a homogenous assay, thereby creating a new immuno-sensing platform for the detection of plant diseases.

All chemicals used in this study were of pure, analytical grade. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were purchased from ACS Materials (USA). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 1-Ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 20 nm colloidal gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), bovine serum albumin (BSA), and phosphate-buffered saline tablets (PBS; 10 mM, pH 7.4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, (St. Louis, MO, USA). Amicon Ultra-4 mL centrifugal filter (50 kDa MWCO) was purchased from EMD Millipore Inc. (Canada). Bacto-peptone and Difco LB Broth Lennox were purchased from BD (USA) and calcium nitrate was purchased from R&M (Malaysia). Bacterial agar was purchased from Oxoid (UK), Quick Start Bradford Dye Reagent/BSA Kit was purchased from Bio-Rad (USA), 96-wells black microplates were purchased from Greiner-Bio (Germany) and Xanthomonas oryzae ELISA detection kit was purchased from LOEWE Biochemica GmbH (Germany).

The antibody produced against Xoo was purified using the Akta-Prime protein purifier system (GE Healthcare). The microscopic analysis was performed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL-JEM 2100F) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FEI-NOVA NANOSEM230). The absorbance spectrum analyses were performed using a UV–visible microplate spectrophotometer (Thermo-Fischer Skan It) and the fluorescence intensity measurements were performed using a TECAN Infinite M − 200 spectrofluorometer (TECAN Austria GmbH) equipped with Magellan software. The data presented in this study represent the average values of three replicate measurements performed for the assays unless stated otherwise.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Production of antibodies against X.oryzae pv. oryzae
ce:section.ce:para: The polyclonal antibodies against Xoo were produced at the AMTREC Animal Laboratory, MARDI using New Zealand white rabbits. The procedures were based on a protocol guideline established with the approval of the Animal Ethics Committee in MARDI (Ref: 2017103/R/MAEC27) [26]. The Xoo cultures (MXO1234, MXO1235 & MXO1186) with high virulence pathotypes were obtained from the Paddy and Rice Research Centre, MARDI. The pathogens were sub-cultured by agar plating using peptone-sucrose agar (PSA) plate media with incubation for 72 h at 30 °C. The mixed-pathotype Xoo cells (1 × 108 CFU mL−1) were emulsified with an equal volume of complete Freund's adjuvant (CFA) and injected subcutaneously at five different sites (200 μL per site) with a total volume of 1 mL. The immunization procedure was continued with three booster injections using incomplete Freund's adjuvant conjugate (IFC) at a weekly interval. The blood samples were obtained from the marginal ear veins of the rabbits after the three booster injections and were subjected to antibody purification methods which include ammonium sulfate precipitation, dialysis, and affinity chromatography. The purified antibodies were stored at 4 °C prior to further analysis.

Water-soluble GQD nanoparticles (1 mg mL−1) were sonicated for 15 min prior to an activation step using carbodiimide. The AntiXoo-GQDs conjugate was prepared by activating 1 mL of GQD nanoparticles with 100 μL of EDC (4 mg mL−1) in 1 mL of 0.01 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The mixed solution was gently stirred for 5 min and was fully activated within 15–20 min at room temperature. Subsequently, 100 μL of NHS (0.15 mg mL−1) was added into the mixed solution and stirred continuously for 20 min. Approximately 1 mL of AntiXoo antibody (1 mg mL−1) was slowly added into the activated GQD solution and the mixture was stirred continuously for 2 h at room temperature. After incubation, 100 μL of 1% BSA solution was added to cover the non-specific sites of the activated GQDs and the unconjugated GQDs were discarded using ultra-filtration (Amicon Ultra-4 mL centrifugal filters, 50 kDa MWCO). The concentrated conjugate was collected and stored at 4 °C until further use.

The conjugation of AntiXoo antibodies with gold nanoparticles was performed using a well-established adsorption method [27]. Firstly, the pH of the AuNPs solution was adjusted to pH 9.0 using 0.2 M of NaOH. Approximately 1 mL of pH-adjusted AuNPs was mixed with 0.1 mL of AntiXoo antibodies (final concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1) and incubated with gentle shaking at room temperature for 1 h. After incubation, 0.1 mL of 10% BSA was added and followed by a 1-h incubation to passivate and stabilize the conjugate. The conjugate mixture was purified by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was discarded and the pellet was re-suspended in 70 μL of 10 mM of PBS (pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C as conjugate stock. The bioconjugates were analysed using UV/Vis absorbance, fluorescence intensity, and electron microscopic analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of X. oryzae pv. oryzae
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the concentration of conjugates pair used in the quenching system, a titration test between GQDs and AUNPs conjugates was performed with constant concentration of Xoo cells at 1 × 105 CFU mL−1. The concentration of probe conjugates ranging between 18 and 45 μg mL−1 (AntiXoo-GQDs) and 4–16 μg mL−1 (AntiXoo-AuNPs). The 1:3 (v/v) dilution of AntiXoo-GQDs and AntiXoo-AuNPs with final concentrations of 23 μg mL−1 and 4 μg mL−1, were selected as optimum probe pairs for the establishment of immuno-sensing system. The detection of Xoo was performed in 96-well black microplates under optimized conditions. In this assay, 60 μL of AntiXoo-GQDs was mixed with 20 μL of the sample (Xoo) and incubated for 25 min at 25 °C. After incubation, 40 μL of AntiXoo-AuNPs was added into the reaction and the solution was topped up to 200 μL of total volume with 0.01 M PBS buffer (pH 7.4). The reaction solution was incubated for another 25 min at 25 °C. The fluorescence intensity was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm and an emission wavelength of 520 nm. The excitation and emission bandwidths were 9 nm and 20 nm, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the sample in the absence and presence of Xoo cells was defined as Fo and Fq, respectively. A calibration curve was developed by plotting the correlation values obtained for the relative changes of fluorescence intensity (ΔF = Fo – Fq/Fo) versus the logarithm of Xoo cells using GraphPad Prism 6 software.

The sensitivity of the immunoassay reaction was determined using a serial concentration of Xoo in sterilized PBS buffer (100–105 CFU mL−1). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on the average value of the blank signal plus three times its standard deviation and determined via the standard calibration curve. The specificity test was performed with the following plant pathogens: Xanthomonas sp. (SS14), Pantoea sp. (DB2), Pseudomonas sp. (TS3), and Pyricularia oryzae (P.o) (1 × 103 CFU mL−1).

Healthy rice plants were cultivated in a controlled environment and used for the analytical performance study. The study was performed on two types of samples; a) Xoo spiked leaf extract and b) artificial inoculated rice leaf. The extraction of rice leaves was optimized to eliminate the interference of the leaf matrix with a signal-to-noise ratio obtained for more than three values (S/N = 3). Approximately 0.1 g of healthy leaves were cut into a smaller size (1 mm2) and spiked with 100 μL of Xoo cells (1 × 103 CFU mL−1) in an Eppendorf tube 1.5 mL. The control sample was spiked with PBS buffer. A volume of 500 μL of PBS buffer (0.01 M pH 7.4) was added into the spiked leaf samples and gently stirred using an Eppendorf tube (1 mL). The homogenized sample was topped up to 1 mL of total volume with PBS buffer and vortexed for 2 min. The leaf debris was discarded and the supernatant was kept at room temperature for 15 min and followed by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 5 min. The pellet was dissolved in 500 μL of PBS and analysed by the developed immunoassay.

In the second analysis, the Xoo infected rice plants were prepared according to the Kauffman method as described by Ke et al. [28]. The leaf samples were harvested at 5, 7, and 14 days after inoculation (DAI) and extracted as previously described. The samples were detected by three different methods; GQDs fluorescence-based immunoassay, indirect ELISA (developed using AntiXoo antibody), and DAS-ELISA (commercial kit-Loewe). All the data were validated using analysis of variance (ANOVA).

The analysis of high recovery probes from the bio-conjugation of AntiXoo antibodies with GQDs and AuNPs nanoparticles is presented in Fig. 2 . GQDs are graphene-based nanomaterial that typically contains carboxyl group on the edge and the conjugation with AntiXoo antibody was achieved by carbodiimide conjugation method in the presence of EDC and NHS. The conjugation has involved covalent binding between the free amine group of antibody and carboxyl groups of GQDs. Fig. 2(a) shows the absorption spectra of the AntiXoo antibody with a typical peak at the wavelength of 280 nm due to the presence of constituent amino acids such as tyrosine, tryptophan, and phenylalanine (aromatic amino acids). Meanwhile, the prominent peak of GQDs absorption spectra at 230 nm has been assigned to the π → π ∗ excitation of the π-bonds. The strong absorption spectra of GQDs in the UV region allows for excitation at any wavelength with a single light source [29]. After the conjugation process, the absorption peak of the AntiXoo-GQDs conjugate shifted to a wavelength of 260 nm due to the modification of the antibody surface, thereby indicating the success of the conjugation process.

The fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs (before conjugation) was observed within the wavelength range of 440 nm–600 nm with a high peak occurring at 520 nm under the excitation at a wavelength of 400 nm (Fig. 2(b)). After conjugation with AntiXoo antibodies, the fluorescence intensity peak at 520 nm wavelength was slightly decreased due to the surface passivation of GQDs in the conjugation process. Instead, the emission spectrum width remains stabled and unshifted because the modification did not increase the diameter size of GQDS through the bioconjugation process. The fluorescence center of GQDs is from surface state induced by hybridization of connected carbon core and edge groups of carboxyl and amide [30,31]. The different emissive sites on each particle of carbon-based fluorescent materials affect the variation of excitation wavelength [32].

Fig. 2(c) shows the UV/Vis spectra of AntiXoo-AuNPs bio-conjugation. The AntiXoo-AuNPs conjugate was produced through electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged AuNPs and the positively charged (amine groups) antibody [27]. The absorption spectra of AntiXoo-AuNPs was slightly shifted towards a higher wavelength (526 nm) as compared to the unconjugated AuNPs (520 nm). The shift in the spectra is due to the interaction of proteins with the surface of AuNPs [33]. A significant fluorescence quenching occurs when the emission spectrum overlaps with the plasmon absorption band of the nanoparticles [22,34]. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the emission fluorescence spectra of AntiXoo-GQDs (Ex. 400 nm) and absorption spectra of AntiXoo-AuNPs was overlapped symmetrically at wavelength peak of 520 nm. This overlap describes the potential of the energy transfer between quantum dots and gold nanoparticles as bio-probe pairs in a fluorescence quenching-based detection system.

FESEM and HRTEM analyses were performed to further examine the morphology and structure of the conjugated probes. In FESEM analysis, the AntiXoo antibody (before conjugation) is viewed as a globular structure consisting of large and small molecules, thereby indicating that the IgG structure contains heavy and light chain polypeptides (Fig. 3 (a)). After conjugation with GQDs, the surface of the AntiXoo antibody is shown to be occupied with small particles that indicate the attachment of GQDs (Fig. 3(b)). Fig. 4(a and b) and 5 (a & b) illustrate the HRTEM images of unconjugated nanoparticles GQDs and AuNPs, and nanoparticles conjugated AntiXoo antibodies, respectively. These purified bioconjugates were stably dispersed in phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.4). The average diameter of these unconjugated nanoparticles was about 15.0 ± 2.5 nm (GQDs) and 19.7 ± 1.5 nm (AuNPs), respectively. Both of these nanoparticles sizes are less than 20 nm, spherical, well separated, and shown monodisperse of narrow-size distribution with high solubility in water that shows their bio-compatibility to conjugate with AntiXoo antibody molecules.

There are various physicochemical methods for the conjugation of nanoparticles to antibody and the covalent binding via amine group is the most common method that suitable for in-vitro application [35]. In this study, conjugation of the AntiXoo antibodies with GQDs through the carbodiimide method was successfully. The bioconjugation of the antibody with GQDs has also been reported in other fluorescence-based immunosensing studies.

The polyclonal antibody against Campylobacter jejuni was successfully conjugated with GQDs via carbodiimide method that supported the strategy of fluorescence probe binding on the surface of bacteria cells membrane for the development of whole-cell FRET immunosensor for analysis in food samples [36]. The conjugation of anti-human immunoglobulin G (mIgG antibody) with GQDs as a donor was also performed to fabricate an LRET immunosensor based on the regulation of the interaction between GQDs and graphene for detecting human immunoglobulin G [37].

The combination bioconjugate probes pair of the fluorescence-labeled and metal quencher-labeled is very important for the superior molecular interaction in the fluorescence-based detection method of Xoo. The selection of AuNPs nanoparticles as metal quencher has been made based on their optoelectronic properties; extraordinary molar extinction coefficients and broad energy absorption bandwidth in the visible range, surface characteristics, shapes, and sizes that make these materials ideal for the analyte detection [38,39]. The AuNPs have a strong surface plasmon field and well-known for ultra-high fluorescence quenching ability. The interaction with the electrons of the fluorophore results in a change of fluorescence emission level, i.e., quenching or enhancement [39,40]. The degree of quenching efficiency increases drastically with an increase in the size of gold nanoparticles from 5 to 70 nm [38]. The metal quencher of AuNPs can be synthesized for diameters size between 2 and 300 nm and their optical absorbance is dependent on the size and shape. The small size of AuNPs up to 40 nm in diameter is expected to quench fluorescence due to the dominant mechanism of absorption, while the larger size above 40 nm is expected to enhance fluorescence based on the dominant mechanism of scattering. Thus, these two criteria determine the ability of gold nanoparticles to induce fluorescence quenching or enhancement.

The different shapes of AuNPs also have different scattering and absorption coefficient that affect the fluorescence quenching or enhancement [41]. The large absorption cross-section near the plasmon resonance of the spherically shaped AuNPs accepts the energy transfer of the donor in any orientation relative to the surface. The shape of spheres in the range of 40–90 nm have high scattering properties. Thus, the induced plasmon will radiate when the scattering overrules the absorption. The large different sizes of AuNPs will results in a different quenching effect and automatically affect the performance of the sensing. The efficient quencher of AuNPs is suggested in diameters below 40 nm and therefore in this study, the size of 20 nm as quencher nanoparticles to GQDs was chosen. The AntiXoo-GQDs and AntiXoo-AuNPs conjugates were obtained at final concentrations of 0.3 mg mL−1 and 0.08 mg mL−1 respectively, as bio-probe stock solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle detection of the turn-off fluorescence GQDs-based immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: The static quenching can also be proved by the analysis of the UV–Vis absorption spectrum [44]. As the formation complex of static quenching occurred at the ground state, it results in the perturbation of the absorption spectrum of fluorophore. This is contrast to dynamic quenching, that no variation occurs in the absorption spectrum at ground-state as it merely affects the excited states of a fluorophore. As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the UV–vis absorption spectrum of AntiXoo-GQDs/Xoo/AntiXoo-AuNPs complex varies at shoulder peak in the range of 230–280 nm compared to AntiXoo-GQDs conjugate alone due to presence of Xoo cells in the immunocomplex binding that describes the chemical constituent of cells (i.e acid amino) [45]. The spectrum red-shifted tailed from 300 nm to 700 nm mainly indicative of the scattering Xoo cells and the absorption peak at 520 nm describes the complex formation. The difference absorption spectrum between AntiXoo-GQDs/Xoo/AntiXoo-AuNPs and GQDs at the same wavelength range confirms the formation of ground-state immunocomplex of static quenching mechanism. Meanwhile, the fluorescence peak spectrum of static quenching is not shifted as the emission involves the unbound fluorophores [46]. As shown in Fig. 8(b), the fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreased almost 30% after the formation of AntiXoo-GQDs/Xoo/AntiXoo-AuNPs complex with a concentration of Xoo cells at 1 × 105 CFU mL−1. The emission peak stably retained at 520 nm and this indicating the static quenching occurred.

The effect of concentration of bio-probe conjugates was investigated for the optimal equilibrium of the immuno-interaction that effectively switch the fluorescence signal and controls the excessive use of the conjugate probes which might encounter the false-positive result (Fig. 9 ). A titration test between AntiXoo-GQDs (P1) and AntiXoo-AuNPs (P2) was performed in various concentrations ranging from 18 to 45 μg mL−1 (P1) and 4–16 μg mL−1 (P2) at a fixed concentration of Xoo cells of 1 × 105 CFU mL−1. The result shows that the use of a high concentration of P1 at 45 μg mL−1 induced only 5–10% of signal quenching. It was speculated that P1 bound to the target cells may accumulate to an excessive level that makes the P2 was less associated with the immuno-complex. By reducing the concentration of P1 to 23 μg mL−1, the quenching efficiency increased up to 30% by the minimum concentration of P2 quencher at 4 μg mL−1 that defined the associated of P2 bound to antigenic sites achieved the optimum equilibrium. Although more amount of P2 might quench the fluorescence even further, a higher concentration of P2 could be less sensitive for the detection of low concentration of Xoo cells. The quenching efficiency reached a plateau in the range of P1 concentrations from 18 to 23 μg mL−1 with a combination pair of P2 concentrations from 4 to 8 μg mL−1. The selection of optimized conjugate pairs has to be considered for the minimal use in reaction assay. Thus, the appropriate concentration of conjugate pairs that enables induce the turn-off switch fluorescence signal of GQDs was optimum at 23 μg mL−1 and 4 μg mL−1 for the concentration of GQDs fluorescence label and AuNPs metal quencher, respectively.

The detection of Xoo was performed under optimum conditions. The changes in fluorescence emission intensity of the turn-off fluorescence immunosensing system using various concentrations of Xoo cells are shown in Fig. 10 (a). When the AntiXoo-GQDs conjugate probe was introduced into the solution contained Xoo cells, the probe was self-regulated binding on the cell surface membrane through the immune recognition between AntiXoo antibody and the antigenic site of Xoo cells. Addition of AntiXoo-AuNPs, the equilibrium randomly binding on the cell surface resulted in the formation of AntiXoo-GQDs/Xoo/AntiXoo-AuNPs ground-state immunocomplex. At the excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the fluorescence quenching occurred and the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was decreased as a turn-off signal. The result indicates the intensity decreased concomitantly with the increase of Xoo cells in the concentrations ranging from 100 to 105 CFU mL−1. The fluorescence spectrum of each concentration of reaction was found asymmetrical at blue-shift suggesting an increase in polarity in the vicinity of QDs due to the interaction within the immunocomplex. However, the high emission peak retained at 520 nm. By increasing the Xoo cells in the reaction system, the aggregation of AntiXoo-GQDs/Xoo/AntiXoo-AuNPs ground-state non-fluorescent immunocomplex increased and the fluorescence signal of GQDs gradually quenched indicates the proximity with high surface plasmon AuNPs. Thus, the change in the fluorescence signal due to the quenching effect by AuNPs was shown to correlate with the increase of cell concentrations. The fluorescence signal induced by the immuno-interaction of antibody-labeled nanoparticles in this homogenous assay permitted the detection of Xoo without washing processes like ELISA that may reduce the specificity and sensitivity of the immunoassay and time-consuming.

The exploration of homogenous fluorescence-based immunoassay concept has brought the possibility of detection in high sensitivity and less time-consuming using labeled specific immuno-binding pair reactions. The combination of GQDs and AuNPs as probes in a fluorescence quenching-based approach was previously reported in various analyte detections such as DNA, metal ion, protein, and cellular imaging [47–49].

Apart from AuNPs, the use of bimetallic nanomaterials such as Ag@AuNPs in GQDs FRET-based sensors was also developed for the detection of plasma glycoproteins [50]. A combination of antibody labeled fluorescence quantum dots with immuno-magnetic separation technique enhanced the homogenous assay approach for the quantitative detection of bacteria E.coli [51]. A homogenous immunoassay using amplified luminescent proximity nanobeads for the procalcitonin protein detection method also demonstrated an excellent analytical performance by the immuno-interaction of antibody-labeled nanoparticles with obvious advantages [52].

Nowadays, the fluorescence-based homogenous concept facilitates more versatile assay in a one-time immunoassay procedure. A selection of donors with large stokes shifts between excitation and emission wavelengths is preferable to eliminate the inner filter effect. As reported by Zhao et al., 2016 [53], the use of europium (Eu) complex in the acceptor beads emitting light at wavelength of 615 nm has proven to minimize the inner filter effects when detecting human epididymis protein 4 (HEP4) in testing serum. Based on the time-resolved mode, the system is free of problems with sample autofluorescence. Therefore, in the proposed method, a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of the donor to sample was determined and the excitation wavelength of GQDs that emitted at wavelength 520 nm with large stokes shift and high SNR value applied in the assay development. Besides, the centrifugation-separation step was performed to discard most of the pigmented-autofluorescence material hence concentrated the target cells. Time-resolved fluoroimmunoassay (TRFI) is traditionally used lanthanide chelate as a label in dissociation-enhancement steps, improvement from the heterogeneous concept to the homogenous with special functionalization of microsphere such as carboxylate-modified polystyrene Eu(III) chelate microparticles (CM-EUs) as labels and magnetic beads as coatings have brought thousands of fluorescent chelates within a single polystyrene shell that enhanced labeling efficiency for a high lanthanide-specific fluorescence signal [54]. The magnetic coatings enable the protection of the chelates from interfering compounds that coexist in the assay media and offer large-surface area of antibody-antigen binding compared to the coating on 96-well microplate solid-phase that limits the interaction of antibody with the antigen. Thus, the antibody-antigen binding can reach dynamic equilibrium in less time and reduces the analytical time. This approach is comparable with the proposed method in terms of detection time which required about 1 h compared to ELISA (>4 h).

The standard calibration curve was constructed based on the relationship between the relative fluorescence change efficiency (ΔF = Fo-Fq/Fo) and the logarithm concentration of Xoo cells (CFU mL−1) in PBS matrix (Fig. 10(b)). A sigmoidal relationship between the signal changes and Xoo cells was observed within the range of 3 × 100–105 CFU mL−1 with the linear portion spanning across the bacterial concentrations of 102–104 CFU mL−1 with correlation coefficient of 0.990. The limit of detection (LOD) is defined as an intensity change in the blank signal plus three times its standard deviation, the calculated LOD of 22 CFU mL−1 was obtained. The sensitivity of the assay increased with the decrease antibody concentration at the optimized equilibrium of the immuno-interaction.

The present research finding was also compared to other immunological methods of Xoo detection. Guo et al. [26] reported the development of Western Blot method using generated Xoo-specific polyclonal antibody. A few major bands of Xoo protein was detected using this method and minimum number of detectable Xoo cells was 3.5 × 103 CFU mL−1. Meanwhile, another method such as Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and immunochromatographic test strip using monoclonal antibody was developed by Wu et al. [55]. The LOD of these both methods were determined at 1 × 104 CFU mL−1 and 1 × 105 CFU mL−1, respectively. It seems that the developed fluorescence-based immunoassay approach has a better limit of detection of Xoo cells as low as 101 CFU mL−1 which offered by excellent properties label of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). In other molecular based approaches such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR), loop-mediated isothermal amplification, and padlock probe assay, the low detection levels of genomic DNA of Xoo were reported at 1 fg μL−1, 1 pg, and 1 pg, respectively [56–58]. However, these methods require well-trained personnel and involve high costs for sophisticated instruments. The developed fluorescence-based method is a highly sensitive method compared to other immuno-based methods for the detection of Xoo. A simple homogenous assay that greatly depends on the high quality of AntiXoo antibodies and the advancement of optical properties of GQDs nanomaterials defined the sensitivity of the detection towards target cells. Thus, the use of GQDs in the developed immunoassay has proven the superior of nanomaterial and biomolecule assemblies in enhancing the plant pathogen detection at the ultra-trace amount that beneficial for the early detection [59].

Several other approaches of fluorescence quenching using quantum dots-conjugated antibodies for pathogen detection have been previously reported. The combination of CdSe/ZnS quantum dots, IgY-AuNPs, and free AuNPs in the facile one-step method detection of V. parahaemolyticus at low concentration of 10 CFU mL−1 based on charge-transfer fluorescence quenching [60]. Likewise, a catalase-mediated fluorescence quenching immunoassay for E-coli detection using antibody-conjugated H2O2-sensitive mercaptopropionic acid-modified CdTe QDs (MPA-QDs) exhibited a LOD value of 5 × 102 CFU mL−1.

The reliability of the immunoassay developed in this study was validated using cross-reactivity studies (Fig. 10(c)). A few plant pathogens comprising of bacteria and fungus at constant concentrations of 103 (CFU mL−1 or spores mL−1), respectively, were tested to elucidate the specificity of the immunoassay. As shown in Fig. 10(c), the turn-off fluorescence immunoassay system obtained 10% of quenching efficiency reacted with Xoo cells. This is high degree quenching efficiency compared to reactivity with other plant pathogens. The low quenching activity less than 2% was obtained for non-target plant pathogens such as Pantoea sp. (papaya), Pseudomonas sp. (paddy), and Pyricularia oryzae (paddy). This result depicts the low cross-reactivity of non-specific binding of other pathogens. The high affinity binding of conjugate probes depending on the quality of antibody used. Approximately 5% of quenching effect towards Xanthomonas sp. (paddy) was obtained due to the bacteria genus similarity. The minimal cross-reactivity observed in this experiment demonstrated the affinity and binding specificity of AntiXoo-GQDs and AntiXoo-AuNPs conjugate bio-probes towards the target cells. The remarkable feature of this specificity is based on the highly specific binding between the antibody and Xoo cells.

In this study, the feasibility of the developed method for Xoo detection was investigated using rice leaf samples. A standard calibration curve for Xoo detection using a leaf matrix was developed and the LOD was calculated to be at 56 CFU mL−1, which was two-fold higher than the PBS matrix (Fig. 10(d)). This observation was probably due to the complex matrix affect the detection system [55]. Since this method is targeted for the early stages of infection, a low concentration of 1 × 103 CFU mL−1 was selected as a concentration spiked in the rice leaf matrix and the result is tabulated in Table 1 . The recovery result of 103.9% was obtained, thereby indicating that the accuracy of this method is applicable for the detection of Xoo in the infected rice leaf samples.

The integration of antibody biomolecule and nanoparticle materials (GQDs and AuNPs) in the fluorescence immuno-based detection approach has enhanced the sensitivity of Xoo detection. The use of high-quality in-house produced AntiXoo antibody that raised against the specific major high virulence pathotype of Xoo is advantageous for the high sensitivity detectable bacterial leaf blight disease infection. The BLB disease commonly attacks rice plants at 60 days after transplant and once the symptoms appear, it indicates the high-accumulation of Xoo infection that normally more than 108 CFU mL−1. Thus, the symptoms of plant diseases have determined the nature of the disease and at this late stage of infection, any detection methods are impractical. However, to control the infection at an early stage, the highly sensitive detection method is essential. Therefore, the present turn-off fluorescence immunoassay method that calibrated with the detection of low range concentration of Xoo cells (100–105 CFU mL−1) has shown a potential approach for the detection at an early stage.

A combination of two nanoconjugates bio-probes (AntiXoo-GQDs and AntiXoo-AuNPs) in a homogenous solution of the turn-off fluorescence quenching system was performed for Xoo cells detection at low concentration. The immunoreaction of probes with target Xoo cells altered the fluorescence intensity of GQDs, thereby resulting in a decrease in signal intensity that was proportional to the increasing of cell concentration. The developed method of Xoo detection have provided several advantages such as; 1) high-quality antibody as bio-detector (in-house produced antibodies specific to Xoo with low cross-reaction with other plant pathogens); 2) green-technology fluorescence-based immuno-sensing platform (carbon-based nanomaterials); 3) highly stable fluorescence label due to low photo-bleaching of GQDs properties; 4) simple approach of a homogenous assay in one mixture solution of self-organizing properties of bio-conjugate probes and target sample without any limitation on the assay format, thus eliminating the laborious steps of coating-washing plate required for the conventional ELISA method, and 5) rapid detection within an hour compared to the conventional microbial culturing plate method that normally takes long times (7–14 days) for the cultivation and enumeration of Xoo colony counting unit. Although the homogenous assay relates to the disadvantages of the false positive or negative result due to high interference and non-specific binding in the solution phase, the improvement of any fluorescence sensing should be emphasized on optimization of the probes concentration, quality control of samples and sample matrix tests to reduce the high signal-to-noise ratio background. Overall, this study offers a new opportunity for nanomaterial-based optical immunoassay with advanced use of graphene quantum dots for the early screening of rice bacterial leaf blight disease in agricultural applications.
